problem_context.title: Two Sum
problem_context.description: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target. You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice.

hint_tests.ai_mentor_hint: Your current solution correctly finds the two numbers! That's a great start. Now, thinking about efficiency, could you find the 'complement' number (the one that adds up to `target` with the current number) more quickly than iterating through the rest of the array each time?

problem_context.title: Valid Parentheses

hint_tests.ai_mentor_hint:

problem_context.title: Container With Most Water
problem_context.description: You are given an integer array height of length n. Find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container, such that the container contains the most water.

hint_tests.ai_mentor_hint:
That's a great start! Your current code correctly calculates the area for every possible pair of lines, which is a very direct way to solve the problem.

When thinking about problems that involve finding an optimal pair of elements in an array, sometimes it helps to consider if you can make progress by working from both ends of the array inwards. How might the potential area change if you were to start with the widest possible container and then decide which side to move next?

problem_context.title: Top K Frequent Elements
problem_context.description: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the k most frequent elements. You may return the answer in any order.

student_input.current_code:
def top_k_frequent(nums, k):
    from collections import Counter
    count = Counter(nums)
    sorted_items = sorted(count.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
    return [item[0] for item in sorted_items[:k]]

hint_tests.ai_mentor_hint: Your use of `Counter` is a great start to efficiently get the frequencies! Now, consider the next step: do you need to sort *all* the unique elements by their frequency, or is there a way to keep track of just the `k` most frequent ones as you process them?

problem_context.title: Product of Array Except Self
problem_context.description: Given an integer array nums, return an array answer such that answer[i] is equal to the product of all the elements of nums except nums[i]. Must run in O(n) without division.

hint_tests.ai_mentor_hint: Your current code correctly calculates the product for each element, which is a great start! To meet the O(n) time complexity requirement, think about how you could pre-calculate the products of elements to the left of `nums[i]` and the products of elements to the right of `nums[i]` more efficiently. Could you build up these products in separate passes?

problem_context.title: Subarray Sum Equals K
problem_context.description: Given an array of integers nums and an integer k, return the total number of continuous subarrays whose sum equals to k.

hint_tests.ai_mentor_hint: Your current approach correctly identifies all subarrays, which is a great start! It's a valid way to solve the problem. For very large arrays, you might find it takes a bit longer. Have you considered if there's a way to keep track of sums more efficiently, perhaps by storing some intermediate results as you iterate through the array?

problem_context.title: Merge Intervals
problem_context.description: Given an array of intervals where intervals[i] = [starti, endi], merge all overlapping intervals.

hint_tests.ai_mentor_hint: You're doing a great job thinking about how to detect and combine overlaps! Your overlap condition looks correct. Sometimes, with problems involving intervals, a particular initial step can make the rest of the logic much simpler. Have you considered if there's a way to arrange the intervals first that might help streamline your merging process?

problem_context.title: Minimum Coin Change
problem_context.description: You are given an integer array coins representing coins of different denominations and an integer amount. Return the fewest number of coins that you need to make up that amount.

hint_tests.ai_mentor_hint:
That's a great start! Your recursive structure correctly identifies the base cases and how to explore different coin choices. You're thinking along the right lines with `dfs`!

As you trace through your `dfs` function, especially for larger `amount`s, consider what happens if the same `rem` value is encountered multiple times during the recursion. Are you recalculating anything unnecessarily?

problem_context.title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
problem_context.description: Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.

hint_tests.ai_mentor_hint: Your recursive approach correctly identifies the choices at each step and the conditions for including an element. That's a great start! As you think about how this function executes, have you considered if you might be solving the same smaller problems multiple times?

problem_context.title: Edit Distance
problem_context.description: Given two strings word1 and word2, return the minimum number of operations required to convert word1 to word2.

hint_tests.ai_mentor_hint: Your recursive logic looks quite insightful and correctly handles the base cases and the different operations. For larger inputs, do you notice any patterns in the `solve(i, j)` calls that might lead to redundant computations?

problem_context.title: Valid Anagram
problem_context.description: Given two strings s and t, return true if t is an anagram of s, and false otherwise.

student_input.current_code:
def is_anagram(s, t):
    return sorted(s) == sorted(t)

hint_tests.ai_mentor_hint: That's a very clever approach! Your current solution using `sorted()` is actually a great way to tackle this problem. Can you explain why comparing the sorted versions of the strings tells you if they are anagrams?

problem_context.title: Find the Duplicate Number
problem_context.description: Given an array of integers nums containing n + 1 integers where each integer is in the range [1, n] inclusive. There is only one repeated number, return it without modifying the array using constant space.

hint_tests.ai_mentor_hint:
That's a good start! Your current code successfully finds the duplicate and already uses constant space, which is excellent given the problem constraints.

Have you considered how the specific range of the integers, from 1 to `n`, might be useful in finding the duplicate without having to compare every number to every other number?
